critic_raw: The design preprocesses missing values by 'forward-filling within 2-hour windows for vitals, then imputing using training-set medians/modes for longer gaps.' For a sequence model that 'learns to handle irregular sampling natively,' forward-filling changes the irregular sampling pattern — it creates synthetic measurements at 2-hour intervals that weren't actually measured. This preprocessing may remove the very signal the sequence model is supposed to capture (irregular sampling patterns as clinical signals). High-frequency measurements may indicate patient concern; absence of measurements may indicate stability. Forward-filling would erase this clinical signal.
defender_raw: The design says the sequence model 'preserves gaps as part of the sequence; the model learns to handle irregular sampling natively.' This means missing measurements are included as gaps in the sequence — the model sees when measurements were and were not taken. The forward-filling within 2-hour windows is a conservative imputation for the aggregated baseline features, not for the sequence model's raw input. The sequence model receives the actual timestamped events with gaps intact. The baseline uses aggregated statistics (mean, min, max) over 24 hours — these aggregates require some imputation for missing values. The two models use different preprocessing appropriate for their architectures.
adjudication_raw: One concern: forward-filling removes irregular sampling signal for the sequence model. Resolved: the design explicitly preserves gaps in the sequence model input; forward-filling is for the baseline aggregated features. The sequence model correctly receives the unmodified irregular event stream. Verdict: defense_wins.
